original: Abd-al-Rahman I becomes emir of Cordova, Spain
Marinus I ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Henry II the Saint crowned King of Italy
Battle of Hexham: English Yorkist troops led by John Neville, Earl of Montagu defeat Lancastrian force headed by Henry Beaufort, Duke of Somerset. Neville has Somerset beheaded.
Cheese & Bread rebellion: German mercenaries kill 232 residents of Alkmaar, Netherlands
Jodocus Badius Ascensius publishes Christiern Pedersen's Latin version of Saxo's "Gesta Danorum" (Deeds of the Danes), in Paris
The battle of Frankenhausen: German peasant army surrounded, 5,000 slaughtered ; ends the peasants' uprising
Louis van Nassau and the Huguenots occupy Valenciennes
Cape Cod discovered by English navigator Bartholomew Gosnold
16 rebellious farmers hanged in Vocklamarkt, Upper-Austria
Treaty of Munster ratified by Spain & Netherlands
1st copyright law enacted by Massachusetts
The War of the Spanish Succession begins
London lawyer James Puckle patents the world's first machine gun
Diego Marín Aguilera flies a glider for "about 360 metres", at a height of 5-6 metres, one of the first attempted flights
France & Sardinia sign Peace treaty of Paris
Ambonese uprising against Dutch authority (modern Indonesia), under Thomas Matulesia (aka Kapitan Pattimura)
First private mental health hospital opens in the US, Asylum for the Relief of Persons Deprived of the Use of Their Reason (now Friends Hospital) in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Francis Baily observes "Baily's Beads" during annular solar eclipse
Philadelphia Turngemeinde founded
2nd San Francisco Vigilance Committee organized
Royal Italian Opera opens in Covent Garden, London
-May 17] Battle of Princeton, West Virginia
Battle of Drewry's Bluff (Fort Darling), Virginia
Confederate ship Alabama launched as the Enrica at Birkenhead, England, where she had been built in secret
First baseball enclosure opens at Union Grounds in Brooklyn
US Department of Agriculture created
Battle of New Market, Virginia
Skirmish at Marksville (Avoyelles) (Red River Campaign)
Dutch government of Zuylen van Nijevelt falls
Italy signs military treaty with Austria-Hungary & Germany
British Central African Protectorate (now Malawi) forms
Operations begin at Philips & Co in Holland
Tornado kills 78 in Texas
Greek army retreats with heavy losses in the Greco-Turkish War
"Bailundo Revolt" begins after Ovimbundu kingdom & allies revolt against Portuguese Empire in Benguela Highlands, central Portuguese Angola
Lyman Gilmore claims to be the first person to fly a powered craft, a steam-powered airplane with a heavy boiler and coal as a power source
Las Vegas founded in Nevada
MLB New York Giants pitcher George "Hooks" Wiltse strikes out 4 Reds batters in 1 inning, as catcher dropped a third strike (Palace of the Fans, in Cincinnati, Ohio)
Reich Association Law comes into force
Last major earthquake on the Elsinore Fault Zone in Southern California
British House of Commons accept Parliament Bill
Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Indiana University, incorporates
Supreme Court dissolves Standard Oil (Sherman Antitrust Act)
The Georgios Averof cruiser is bought by Greece
Bolivia becomes a signatory to the Buenos Aires copyright treaty.
Henri Rabaud's opera "Mârouf, savetier du Caire" (Marouf, the Cairo Cobbler) premieres in Paris

US Colonel Edward House sails for Europe to persuade major powers to reduce armies and navies; from Germany, House reports: 'Everybody's nerves are tense; it only needs a spark to set the whole thing off'
Asiago, Italy, falls when Austrian troops attack the Italian front
Claiming that the USA must act to quell dangerous disorder, the government orders US Marines to land in Santo Domingo; the American occupation will continue until 1924
The first officer's training camp is opened in the US, as the country prepares for war
1st regular US airmail postal service between NY, Philadelphia & Washington, D.C.
Finnish Civil War ends
Greeks troops land at the Ottoman-held city of Smyrna (Izmir) beginning the Greco-Turkish War
Washington SenatorWalter Johnsonpitches 1-0, 18 inning game
Beginning of the Winnipeg General Strike as more than 30,000 workers walk off the job in the largest strike in Canadian history (ends 25 June)
Cincinnati Reds score 10 runs in top of the 13th to beat The Brooklyn Robins, 10-0 at Ebbets Field in Brooklyn, New York City
Soccer team ADO '20 forms in Heemskerk
British Legion formed to care for ex-servicemen
Germany turns over the Upper Silesia region to Poland under Allied pressure and despite a plebiscite in favor of merging with Germany
Cooperation of Dutch Molen forms
Mickey Mouse makes his first appearance in the silent film "Plane Crazy"
Fire in X-ray film stock kills 125 at Crile Clinic (Cleveland, Ohio)
Ellen Church becomes 1st female airline stewardess aboard a United flight from San Francisco to Cheyenne
The 15th May Incident: in an attempted coup d'état, the Prime Minister of Japan Inukai Tsuyoshi is shot and killed by Navy officers in his home
1st voice amplification system to be used in US Senate
Karlis Ulmanis names himself fascist dictator of Latvia
US Department of Justice offers $25,000 reward for Dillinger, dead or alive
MLB Pittsburgh Pirates squeak past Phillies 20-5 at Philadelphia's Baker Bowl
Moscow Metro opens to public; initial phase was 11 km (6.8 mi) long and included 13 stations
Paul-Henri Spak forms red coalition of Belgium
German armoured division moves into Northern France
German troops occupy Amsterdam, General Winkelman surrenders
Nazis capture General Dutch Persbureau (ANP)
Richard and Maurice McDonald open the first McDonald's restaurant in San Bernardino, California
USS Sailfish (SS-192) recommissioned, originally the Squalus
1st British turbojet flies
English engineer Frank Whittle successfully tests his design for the first turbojet engine aboard a Gloster E.28/39 flying from RAF Cranwell
WWII: British attack Halfaya-pass in Egypt and Fort Capuzzo in Italian Libya
WWII: Nazi occupiers in Netherlands forbid Jewish music
Gasoline 1st rationed in US (17 Eastern States)
WWII: Nazi occupiers in Netherlands arrest 2,000 Dutch officers
Halifax bombers sinks U-463
Cincinnati Red Clyde Shoun no-hits Boston Braves, 1-0
Sergei Aleksi becomes guardian of the Patriarch Throne of the Russian Orthodox Church
World War II: The final skirmish in Europe is fought near Prevalje, Slovenia
Australia scores 721 runs in one day v Essex, world record
End of the British Mandate over Palestine after 28 years
Troops from Egypt, Lebanon, Syria, Iraq and Saudi Arabia attack Israel
Adnan Menderes elected Prime Minister of Turkey in the country's first democratic elections
AT&T becomes the first US corporation to have a million stockholders after young car salesman Brady Denton purchases seven shares worth $1,078
Polish cultural attache in Paris, Czesław Miłosz, asks the French government for political asylum
Detroit Tiger Virgil Trucks no-hits Washington Senators, 1-0
Johnny Longdenbecomes 2nd jockey to ride 4,000 winners
Osip Zadkine's monument to "The destroyed city" unveiled in Rotterdam
The Urey-Miller experiment using primordial soup to show how life of earth might have originated, written by Stanley Miller, is published in the journal "Science"
KGLO (now KIMT) TV channel 3 in Mason City, IA (CBS) 1st broadcast
Austrian State Treaty: US, Britain, France and the Soviet Union restore Austria's independence
Construction begins on the Soviet space travel center at Baikonur in Kazakhstan
First ascent of Makalu, the world's fifth-highest mountain on the China-Nepal border in the Himalayas
KPUA (now KGMD) TV channel 9 in Hilo, HI (CBS) begins broadcasting
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
The Soviet Union tests the world's first intercontinental ballistic missile, the R-7, crashes prematurely after 400 km (250 mi)
USSR launches Sputnik III
100th anniversary reenactment of the first college baseball game between Amherst and Williams
12th Cannes Film Festival: "Black Orpheus" directed by Marcel Camus wins the Palme d'Or
"L'Avventura", Italian film directed by Michelangelo Antonioni, starring Gabriele Ferzetti, Monica Vitti. premieres at Cannes
Chic Cub Don Cardwell no-hits St Louis Cards, 4-0
KHVO TV channel 13 in Hilo, HI (ABC) begins broadcasting
Sputnik 4 launched into Earth orbit; later recovery failed
"Bonanza" by Al Caiola Orchestra hits #19
36 Unification church couples wed in Korea
US marines arrive in Laos
Folksinging trio Peter, Paul & Mary win their 1st Grammy for cover version ofPete Seeger's song "If I Had a Hammer"
Last Project Mercury flight launched, Gordon Cooper in Faith 7
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Canadian Football Players Association organizes
Igor Vodic beats Mad Dog Vachon in Omaha, to become NWA champ
South Vietnamese army battle Buddhists, about 80 die
1st AL game played in Milwaukee, is a 4-2 California win against Chicago
Paul McCartney& John Lennon appear on "The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson", to promote Apple Records, Joe Garagiola is substitute host
Tornado strikes Jonesboro, Arkansas at 10 PM, killing 36
Associate Justice Abe Fortas resigns from Supreme Court
Elizabeth Hoisington and Anna Mae Mays named 1st female US generals
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
International Olympic Committee votes to expel South Africa
Mississippi Highway Patrol kill 2 students during racial disturbance at Jackson State University in Mississippi
Irish Republican Army member William 'Billy' Reid is shot dead by British soldiers in Belfast
Radio Nordsee International's ship bombed
Bus plunges into Nile River killing 50 pilgrims in Minia, Egypt
Ryukyu Is & Daito Is returned to Japan after 27 yrs of US control
The island of Okinawa, under U.S. military governance since its conquest in 1945, reverts to Japanese control.
California AngelNolan Ryan's 1st no-hitter beats KC Royals, 3-0

Beginning of the Ulster Workers' Council strike called by Ulster loyalists and unionists who were against the Sunningdale Agreement, which proposed the sharing of political power with Irish nationalists
Ma'alot massacre: Palestinian terrorist take school hostage, 25 mostly children killed and 68 injured
Walter Scheel succeeds Gustav Heinemann as West German President
"Fonz Song" by Heyettes hits #91
Kentucky Moonrunner by Cledus Maggard hits #85
The Provisional Irish Republican Army kill 3 Royal Ulster Constabulary (RUC) officers in County Fermanagh and 1 in County Down

The Ulster Volunteer Force launch gun and bomb attacks on 2 pubs in County Armagh, killing 4 Catholic civilians and wounding many more; a British Army soldier is later convicted for taking part in the attacks
Flyers score 8 goals against Islanders in playoffs
George Harrisonreleases single "All Those Years Ago" in UK: the song was a tribute to John Lennon, and featured Ringo Starr on drums, and Paul and Linda McCartney on backing vocals
Len Barker of Cleveland pitches perfect game vs Toronto
SCTV Network 90, sequel to Second City Television, debuts on NBC
Soyuz 40 carries 2 cosmonauts (1 Romanian) to Salyut 6
Madison Hotel (Boston) destroyed by implosion
1st Energiya Launch (USSR)
Record archery score for a pair over 24 hrs, is set
The Soviet Union begins withdrawing its 115,000 troops from Afghanistan
Maxwell House coffee runs ads during "Roe vs Wade" movie despite threat of boycott by right-to-lifers

After years of disagreement and political struggle,King Johnof England yields to Rome and allows Stephen Langton to return and become Archbishop of Canterbury. The crisis is a major factor that led to the Magna Carta in 1215.
Mongolian leaderGenghis Khanmeets the leader of the Taoist Dragon Gate sect Master Changchun (Qiu Chuji) at his camp in Parwan near the Hindu Kush in Afghanistan
PopeInnocent IVissues the papal bull Ad Extirpanda, which authorizes but also limits the torture of heretics in the Medieval Inquisition
Parliament of Paris appoints 8-year oldLouis XIIIas French King, following the assassination of his fatherHenry IV;  Louis' mother and Henry's widow Marie de' Medici serves as regent until 1617
German astronomerJohannes Keplerdiscovers the third of his three planetary laws, the "harmonic law"
PopeAlexander VIIappoints committee to investigate Jansenism
Alexander Pope's "An Essay on Criticism" is published anonymously
Maximilien Robespierreproposes the self-denying ordinance, making it so that no deputy who sat in the Constituent Assembly could sit in the succeeding Assembly
First Coalition:Napoleonenters Milan in triumph with French troops
KingGeorge IIIsurvives a second assassination attempt
Pope Pius VIIcalls on French bishops to return to Gospel principles
Joseph Smithordained by John the Baptist according to Joseph Smith
KingMongkut[Rama IV or Phra Chomklao Chaoyuhua] crowned in Siam (Thailand)
Major GeneralBenjamin Butlerissues order (New Orleans) that Confederate women abusing Union soldiers be treated as whores
Salon des Refusés opens in Paris, exhibition of works rejected by official Salon, featuresPaul Cézanne,Camille Pissarro, Henri Fantin-Latour, James Whistler, and Édouard Manet
National Woman Suffrage Association forms in New York, founded bySusan B. AnthonyandElizabeth Cady Stanton
May Laws: Russian TsarAlexander IIIbans Jews from living in rural Romania
Canadian Métis insurgentLouis Rielcaptured in the aftermath of the Battle of Batoche in Saskatchewan
British sailorDavid Beattyis promoted to midshipman
Jules Massenet's opera "Grisélidis" premieres in Paris
PopeLeo XIIIpublishes encyclical Rerum novarum
The Scientific-Humanitarian Committee is founded in Berlin byMagnus Hirschfeld, the first-ever LGBT rights organization
Pierre Savorgnan de Brazzareaches Leopoldville in the Congo
Ty Cobbrushes a heckler at a NY Highlander game & is suspended
Italian General Election: National Bloc, includingMussolini's fascists 35 seats, win most seats but not a majority
Hollywood landmark the Roosevelt Hotel opens in Los Angeles byDouglas Fairbanks,Mary Pickford, and Sid Grauman, site of the first Academy Awards ceremony in 1929
PopePius XIpublishes the encyclical "Quadragesimo Anno" on the dangers to human freedom and dignity from unrestrained capitalism, socialism, and communism
Amy Johnsonarrives in Croydon, England having flown from South Africa in a record 4 days and 16 hours
Buck O'Neilmakes his debut playing for the Negro league baseball team Kansas City Monarchs at Ruppert Stadium, Kansas City
New York YankeesJoe DiMaggiostarts 56-game hitting streak, with the only RBI, in a 13-1 loss to visiting Chicago White Sox
Joseph Stalindissolves the Comintern (or Third International) to avoid upsetting his Western allies with claims he was trying to foment revolution globally
14,000 Jews of Munkacs, Hungary, deported toAuschwitz
Dwight D. Eisenhower,Bernard Montgomery,Winston Churchilland KingGeorge VIdiscuss the plan for D-Day
First vaginoplasty procedure (sex reassignment surgery) in the UK performed on Roberta Cowell byHarold Gillies
In his first world heavyweight title defense,Rocky MarcianoKOs former championJersey Joe Walcottin the 1st round at Chicago Stadium
EvangelistBilly Grahamlaunches his "crusade" in front of 18,000 people at Madison Square Garden in NYC
"Gigi" based on the story byColette, directed by Vincent Minnelli, and starring Leslie Caron and Maurice Chevalier premieres in New York City (Best Picture 1959)
Dmitri Shostakovich's 7th String quartet premieres in Leningrad
PopeJohn XXIIIpublishes encyclical "Mater et Magistra" on Christianity and social progress
5th Grammy Awards: "I Left My Heart In San Francisco";Robert Gouletwin
Paul McCartneymeets his future wife, American photographerLinda Eastman
Joe Massot's psychedelic feature "Wonderwall". with music byGeorge Harrison, premieres at Cannes Film Festival
Assassination attempt on US GovernorGeorge Wallaceof Alabama by Arthur Bremer in Laurel, Maryland
TV comedy film "The Harlem Globetrotters onGilligan's Island" airs on NBC
Former President of ArgentinaLeopoldo Galtieriis sentenced to 12 years in prison for mishandling the Falkland Islands War
Last episode of "The Late Show withJoan Rivers" after the host is fired by the Fox network
Anne Boleynand her brother George, Lord Rochford, accused of adultery and incest
First emigrant wagon train to reach California leaves Independence, Missouri, on a 1,730-mile journey over the Sierra Nevada
Operation Grapple: Britain tests its first hydrogen bomb near Christmas Island in the Indian Ocean
Chungmok of Goryeo, 29th King of the Goryeo Dynasty of Korea (1344-48), born in Korea (d. 1348)

Andrew of Austria, Austrian nobleman (Margrave of Burgau), clergyman (Catholic cardinal and bishop), and Governor-General of the Habsburg Netherlands (1598-1599), born in Bresnitz Castle, Březnice, Bohemia (now Czech Republic) (d. 1600)
Henrick de Keyser, Dutch architect and sculptor (master builder of Amsterdam, Zuiderkerk church), born in Utrecht (d. 1621)
Claudio Monteverdi, Italian composer and pioneer in development of opera (L'Orfeo, espro della Beata Vergine), baptized in Cremona, Italy (d. 1643)
René Goupil, French Jesuit missionary and first canonized Catholic martyr in Canada, born in Saint-Martin-du-Bois, Kingdom of France (d. 1642)[1]
Mary Wortley Montagu, English poet and writer (wrote about life in Istanbul, advocated for smallpox inoculation), born at Holme Pierrepont Hall, England (d. 1762) (baptism 26 May)
Edward Wortley Montagu, English traveler and writer, born in England (d. 1776)
Maximilian Hell [Höll], Slovakian astronomer, Jesuit priest, and namesake of a lunar crater, born in Selmecbánya, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1792)
Levi Lincoln, Sr., American revolutionary, statesman, politician, and Thomas Jefferson's first attorney general, born in Massachusetts Bay, British America (d. 1820)
Pierre Dutillieu, French-Italian opera composer, born in Lyon, France (d. 1798)
Maria Theresia von Paradis, Austrian blind pianist, singer, organist, and composer, born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1824)

Baron Floris van Hall, Dutch nobleman and politician (Prime Minister 1853-56, and 1860-61), born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (now Netherlands) (d. 1866)

Gottfried Herrmann, German violinist, organist, Kapellmeister, and composer, born in Sondershausen, Confederation of the Rhine (now Germany) (d. 1878)
Michael William Balfe, Irish violinist and composer (The Bohemian Girl), born in Dublin (d. 1870)
Jacob Thompson, American politician, 5th United States Secretary of the Interior (1857-61), born in Leasburg, North Carolina (d. 1885)
Stephen Heller, Hungarian composer and pianist, born in Pest (d. 1888)
Debendranath Tagore, Indian philosopher of the Bengal Renaissance, religious reformer and founder of the Brahmo religion, born in Calcutta, British India (d. 1905)

Daniel Ammen, American naval officer, Chief of the Bureau of Navigation (Civil War) and author, born in Brown County, Ohio (d. 1898)
Laurence S. Baker, American officer and brigadier general in the Confederate States Army (Civil War), born in Gates County, North Carolina (d. 1907)
Giovanni Bolzoni, Italian violinist and composer, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma (now Italy)  (d. 1919)
Ilya Mechnikov, Russian zoologist and bacteriologist known as the "father of natural immunity" (Nobel 1908), born in Ivanovka, Kharkov Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1916)
Viktor Vasnetsov, Russian painter, considered co-founder of Russian folklorist and romantic nationalistic painting, born in Lopyal, Russian Empire(d. 1926)
Herbert Lawford, British tennis player (Wimbledon 1887; first exponent of topspin), born in Bayswater, England (d. 1925)
Eduard von Keyserling, Baltic German writer (Dritte Stiege), born in Schloss Tels-Paddern, Courland Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1918)
Williamina Fleming, pioneering Scottish astronomer, discovered the Horsehead Nebula (1888), born in Dundee, Scotland (d. 1911)
Pierre Curie, French physicist (Nobel 1903) and husband of Marie Curie, born in Paris, France (d. 1906)
Ellen Axson Wilson (née Ellen Louise Axson), American artist and First Lady (US, 1913-14; New Jersey, 1911-13), 1st wife of US President Woodrow Wilson, born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 1914)
Arthur Schnitzler, Austrian playwright (La Ronde - Reigen), and novelist (Fräulein Else), born in Leopoldstadt, Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1931)
John Reeves Ellerman, British accountant, businessman, and shipping magnate, born in Kingston upon Hull, England (d. 1933)
Vilhelm Hammershøi, Danish painter known for his subdued interiors and portraits, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1916)
Albert Verwey, Dutch poet and literary historian (Motion), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1937)
Lee Stack, British Army Major-General, Egyptian Army Sirdar, and Governor-General of Sudan (1917-24), born in Darjeeling, British India (d. 1924)
Nikolay Tcherepnin, Russian composer of ballets, born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1945)
Francisco Pujol, Catalan singer, choirmaster (Orfeó Català), composer, and musicologist, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1945)

Bill Williams [Herman Wilhelm Katt], American actor (The Adventures of Kit Carson; Date With the Angels), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1992)
Hilda Bernstein, British-South African author, artist, and activist, born in London (d. 2006)[1]
Paul A. Samuelson, American economist (1970 Nobel Prize for Economics, 1947 John Bates Clark Medal), born in Gary, Indiana (d. 2009)[1]

Charles Palmer, English cricket batsman (1 Test; Leicestershire CCC, Worcestershire CCC) and administrator (President MCC 1978–79, chairman Test and County Cricket Board 1983-85), born in Sandwell, England (d. 2005)
